home.title: Cancún Sargassum Today — Live Seaweed Map & 7-Day Forecast 2026
home.desc: Which Cancún or Riviera Maya beach is free of sargassum today? Live per-beach seaweed map, Beach Score 0-100 and a 7-day forecast. Updated 4× a day from satellite.

faq.q: When is sargassum season in Cancún and the Riviera Maya?
faq.a: The season runs April through September, peaking from May to July. November through March the beaches are usually clean, though stray patches can land at any time. Because each year is different — sometimes wildly so — we track every beach with Copernicus satellite imagery refreshed four times a day, so you see what is on the sand today rather than a historical average.

faq.q: Which Riviera Maya beaches get the least sargassum?
faq.a: The ones that don't face east: Playa Norte on Isla Mujeres, Holbox at the top of the peninsula, and the west coast of Cozumel (Palancar) almost never get sargassum. Along the Cancún–Tulum corridor, beaches sheltered by a bay or reef — Akumal, Puerto Morelos — pile up far less than the open ones. Check the live map: daily satellite data shows which beaches are seaweed-free right now.

faq.q: How reliable is the data on this site?
faq.a: We use the AFAI index — the same standard NOAA uses to detect floating sargassum from satellite — with Copernicus imagery refreshed four times a day. Reports from people on the beach fill in what clouds can hide. Every beach shows the exact time of its last update and a confidence level, so you know how fresh the reading you're looking at really is.

faq.q: Can you forecast sargassum a week ahead?
faq.a: Yes, within reasonable limits. Sargassum drifts with the wind and currents, so once the satellite spots a mat offshore we can project its path toward each beach over the next seven days. Days 1 to 3 are the most accurate; days 4 to 7 show the trend. The forecast is recalculated every day with each new satellite pass.

faq.q: Do the hotels clean sargassum off their beaches?
faq.a: The big resorts in Cancún, Playa del Carmen and Tulum rake their beachfront every morning, and many install offshore sargassum barriers; the municipalities and the Navy also collect tons in season. Even so, on a heavy landing day the seaweed comes back within hours. That's why it pays to watch the real satellite flow: if nothing new is inbound, a raked beach stays clean all day.

faq.q: What do I do if my beach is buried in sargassum?
faq.a: Move — the geography gives you options. If the Cancún hotel zone wakes up bad, Playa Norte on Isla Mujeres is usually clean; from Playa del Carmen, the ferry puts you half an hour from Palancar on Cozumel. Fresh sargassum isn't dangerous, but rotting piles smell of sulfur and are best avoided. With premium alerts we tell you the moment your beach changes status.

forecast.title: Cancún Sargassum Forecast 2026 — 7-Day Outlook by Beach
forecast.desc: 7-day sargassum forecast for Cancún, Playa del Carmen and Tulum, updated daily from satellite. See which Riviera Maya beaches will be clean this week.
forecast.h1: Sargassum Forecast for Cancún & the Riviera Maya — Next 7 Days
forecast.intro: Most sargassum forecasts for the Mexican Caribbean hand you one verdict for all of Quintana Roo, built from official imagery that's already days old by the time it's published. This works differently: we run a separate forecast for each of the 12 beaches we monitor, from Holbox to Mahahual, because reality changes from one bay to the next — the same mat that buries Playa Delfines can slide right past Puerto Morelos. Every morning the model ingests the latest Copernicus satellite pass, locates the mats drifting inbound, and projects landings for the next seven days, beach by beach. The result updates daily — four satellite reads a day feed it — so you can pick your beach day before the sargassum picks it for you.
forecast.method: We start from the AFAI (Alternative Floating Algae Index), the spectral signature Copernicus and NOAA satellites use to detect floating sargassum from orbit. Each detected mat is projected forward with wind and surface-current data — sargassum drifts at roughly 2.5% of wind speed plus the local current. For seaweed already on the sand we apply exponential persistence with a 3.5-day half-life: it decays unless fresh material arrives. Community reports correct the satellite when clouds get in the way. Accuracy is highest on days 1 to 3, and everything recomputes daily.

today.intro: Looking for a seaweed-free beach today? Below is the live list, ranked cleanest to most affected, showing which beaches are sargassum-free right now on the latest satellite pass — refreshed four times a day. As a rule of thumb, Playa Norte, Holbox and the west side of Cozumel almost always top the list, while the Cancún–Tulum corridor rises and falls with the week's wind. But the rule of thumb isn't what matters once the cooler is packed: today's reading is. Check the number next to your beach and go.

map.h1: Live Seaweed & Sargassum Map — Cancún and the Riviera Maya
map.intro: This is an interactive, live map of the sargassum — the seaweed — across the Mexican Caribbean: every beach, from Holbox to Mahahual, shown with a color-coded status (clean, moderate, or avoid). It's fed by Copernicus satellite imagery refreshed four times a day, plus reports from people standing on the sand right now. Tap any beach for its current status, a 0–100 Beach Score, and the 7-day forecast. Unlike a seasonal guide, this map changes when the sea changes: an overnight wind shift can clear Cancún and load Tulum by noon. Check it the morning you go.

season.intro: Planning a Riviera Maya trip and worried about sargassum and seaweed? Here's the honest 2026 picture — when the seaweed arrives, when the beaches stay clean, and how to time your trip. Short version: November through March is reliable and clean; the Atlantic belt arrives April through October, heaviest May to August. If your dates are flexible, that single fact plans the trip.
season.title: Cancún Sargassum Season 2026 — Month-by-Month Guide
season.desc: When does sargassum hit Cancún and the Riviera Maya? April to September, peaking May–July. Month-by-month 2026 guide with daily satellite tracking.
season.h1: Sargassum Season in Cancún & the Riviera Maya — 2026 Guide

months.text: The reliable window. Landings are rare and light, the Atlantic is at its annual minimum, and almost the entire coast — Cancún to Tulum — stays clean for weeks at a stretch. If avoiding sargassum is your priority, travel in these months.

The warning month. Satellites start picking up the first mats of the new bloom crossing the Atlantic. Beaches are mostly still clean, but stray patches can land on the open coast. Worth a glance at the map before you book tours.

The season kicks off. Landings become regular on the east-facing beaches — Playa del Carmen, Tulum, Xpu-Ha — though still at moderate volumes that crews handle in the morning. Isla Mujeres, Holbox and west Cozumel stay quiet.

The peak. The Atlantic sargassum belt delivers steady volume and the open coast can wake up buried several times a week. The gap between beaches becomes enormous: on the same day, Delfines can be brown and Palancar crystal-clear. This is when a per-beach forecast is worth its weight in gold.

Still strong but easing. Landings turn episodic: a beach can be covered Tuesday and raked clean by Friday. Hotels keep up the cleaning pace and sheltered beaches recover fast. The 7-day radar helps you dodge the pulses.

The tail of the season. Volumes drop week by week and by month's end most beaches are stably clean again. A late pulse can sneak in after a norte, so the cautious keep checking the map — but the odds are firmly in your favor.

season.tips: If your dates are flexible, book November through March and forget about seaweed. Traveling in peak season? Favor staying on Isla Mujeres, Holbox or the west side of Cozumel — or, in the corridor, pick a resort with daily cleaning and an offshore barrier. Check our map the week before you fly (last month's forum posts are useless), and once you're here, plan beach mornings and keep a cenote or a Cozumel day as your plan B for the heavy days.

beachBlurbs.rm001: Cancún's most famous public beach, with the lookout by the giant sign and no hotels blocking the view. It faces dead east with strong surf, so in season it's among the first to catch sargassum — and being public, cleanup depends on the municipality. Go early on a green-flag day; the postcard is worth it.
beachBlurbs.rm002: A family-friendly beach in the northern hotel zone, with calm water thanks to the shelter of Isla Mujeres and the ferry pier a step away. Its north-facing orientation spares it much of the sargassum that hammers the rest of Cancún. Ideal with kids and easy to pair with a hop over to the island.
beachBlurbs.rm003: The quintessential town beach: step off the bus, cross Fifth Avenue, and you're on the sand, with the Cozumel ferry right there. It faces east and takes moderate sargassum in season, though municipal and beach-club cleaning is constant. Perfect if you want beach without giving up restaurants and nightlife.
beachBlurbs.rm004: The Tulum postcard: leaning palms, white sand, and the most photographed beach clubs in Mexico. It's fully exposed to the east, so at peak season it can wake up loaded — each club rakes its stretch daily. Arrive early for the cleanest sand and the best spot under the palapa.
beachBlurbs.rm005: The turtle bay: shallow water, a reef that breaks the swell, and snorkeling with turtles a few meters off the shore. The shape of the bay holds less sargassum than the open beaches, though a heavy landing still gets some through. One of the corridor's safer bets for families and snorkelers.
beachBlurbs.rm006: The good side of Cozumel: the west, leeward coast, where sargassum barely reaches even at peak season. Clear water, a relaxed beach club, and the Palancar reef for snorkeling a few minutes away by boat. The perfect plan B when the corridor wakes up brown — and a great day out in its own right.
beachBlurbs.rm007: The jewel of Isla Mujeres and one of the few Mexican Caribbean beaches that almost never sees sargassum, thanks to its north-facing orientation. Waist-deep water for hundreds of meters, calendar-worthy sunsets, and a small-town feel. When everything else fails in season, Playa Norte delivers.
beachBlurbs.rm008: A fishing village halfway between Cancún and Playa del Carmen, with the national-park reef just 500 meters offshore. The reef blocks part of the sargassum and the vibe stays boardwalk-and-boat, not nightclub. For travelers after top-tier snorkeling and a slower Mexican pace.
beachBlurbs.rm009: The deep south of the Costa Maya: a town boardwalk, shallow water, and prices that are still friendly. Sargassum is variable with the wind, but the boardwalk stretch is cleaned daily and, outside cruise-ship days, the beach is nearly private. For travelers who want the Caribbean without the corridor's scale.
beachBlurbs.rm010: Up at the top of the peninsula, already in Gulf waters, sargassum simply barely reaches — that's half the magic. The other half: sand streets, flamingos, summer bioluminescence, and endless sandbars. The anti-sargassum escape par excellence — no cars, no rush.
beachBlurbs.rm011: A wide, still-relaxed bay between Playa del Carmen and Tulum, with white sand, fried-fish palapas, and far less noise than its neighbors. It's exposed to the east, so in season it's worth checking on the map before you drive out. On a clean day it competes with any beach in the corridor.
beachBlurbs.rm012: Ranked for years among the most beautiful beaches in the world, today it's luxury-resort territory with talcum sand and turquoise water. The hotels invest heavily in barriers and daily cleaning, so it usually wakes up better than the open coast in season. For honeymoons and spa days by the sea.

press._doc: Press/media page rendered at /en/press/ by region-seo-pages.cjs. Tokens resolved at build time from real data — never invented. Mirror of puntacana press contract.
press.title: Cancún & Riviera Maya Sargassum — Press Kit
press.desc: Press resources for sargassum coverage in Cancún and the Riviera Maya: live per-beach satellite status refreshed 4× daily, 7-day forecasts and published accuracy. Free to cite.
press.h1: Press & media — Riviera Maya sargassum data
press.intro: The Atlantic is having a widely reported record sargassum season. What the headlines rarely answer is the question your readers actually ask: is my beach swimmable today? We publish that answer for Cancún and the Riviera Maya, beach by beach, from satellite data — and it is free to cite with attribution.

sections.text: Live sargassum status for {beachCount} beaches across Cancún and the Riviera Maya — from Playa Delfines and Isla Mujeres' Playa Norte down to Akumal, Puerto Morelos, Xpu-Ha and Mahahual — each with a clean / moderate / avoid rating, a 0–100 Beach Score and a beach-specific 7-day forecast. Detection uses the AFAI (Alternative Floating Algae Index) from the Copernicus and NOAA satellite programs, sampled pixel by pixel directly offshore of each beach — not a regional average — and refreshed four times a day, with wind and swell data from Open-Meteo. As of {updatedDate}, {cleanToday} of {beachCount} tracked beaches read clean.

We backtest the forecast against what satellites later observed, and we publish the results: across our network, next-day beach status verifies {accuracyJ1Pct}% of the time, and 3-day status {accuracyJ3Pct}%. Days 1–3 are the most reliable; days 4–7 show the trend — and we say so on every page. The data is never hand-edited, never filtered for resorts, never deleted.

Journalists, bloggers and broadcasters may quote any beach status, score or forecast with a link to the source page. Suggested citation: {citation}. For an embed of the live map, beach-level data for a story, or comment on current conditions, write to {contact} — we answer quickly.

Which Riviera Maya beach is clean this week (live ranked list, updated 4× daily) · Why Isla Mujeres, Cozumel and Holbox often escape the sargassum that hits Tulum and Playa del Carmen · What a record Atlantic bloom means at beach level for Quintana Roo tourism · How satellite AFAI detection works, with measured forecast accuracy published on our methodology page.

SargassumCancun.com is part of a five-site monitoring network: Martinique and Guadeloupe (French), Punta Cana (English), Cancún & Riviera Maya (Spanish), and Miami & Florida (English). All five sites run the same satellite pipeline and the same openly published methodology. The live map is free for everyone; a premium subscription adds daily beach recommendations, push alerts and a morning brief.

press.citation: Source: sargassumcancun.com — Copernicus/NOAA satellite data, refreshed 4× daily

extraPages.h1: Cancun Seaweed Map 2026 — Live Sargassum, Beach by Beach
extraPages.intro: The Cancun seaweed map for 2026 is a live, interactive map showing the sargassum on every beach from Holbox to Mahahual right now — each one rated clean, moderate, or avoid, with a 0–100 Beach Score and a 7-day forecast. It is updated four times a day from Copernicus and NOAA satellite imagery, so what you see is today's water, not last season's average. That is the difference. Most "2026 sargassum maps" hand you one verdict for all of Quintana Roo, built from imagery that is already days old. This one reads each beach separately, pixel by pixel offshore — because the same mat that buries Playa Delfines can slide right past Puerto Morelos. We are honest about uncertainty: every beach shows the exact time of its last update and a confidence level, and the forecast is sharpest on days 1–3. Tap your beach, check its status today, and go.

sections.h2: Which Cancun & Riviera Maya beaches are clean in 2026?
sections.text: The map ranks every tracked beach cleanest-first, but the geography rarely lies: the beaches that don't face east take the least sargassum. Playa Norte on Isla Mujeres, Holbox at the top of the peninsula, and Cozumel's leeward west coast (Palancar) stay clear even at peak — their orientation does the work. Along the open Cancun–Tulum corridor, a bay or reef helps: Akumal and Puerto Morelos pile up far less than exposed stretches like Playa Delfines, Playa del Carmen and Tulum's beach-club strip. But a rule of thumb is not today's reading. An overnight wind shift can clear Cancun and load Tulum by noon, so check each beach's status today on the live map before you pack the cooler — the Beach Score next to each name tells you which water is genuinely turquoise this morning.

sections.h2: When does the seaweed clear, and is it dangerous?
sections.text: Sargassum season runs April through September, peaking May to July; November through March the coast is usually clean for weeks at a stretch. On any single beach, fresh seaweed decays once no new mats arrive — a stretch buried Tuesday can be raked clear by Friday, especially where hotels run daily cleaning and offshore barriers. Fresh sargassum on the sand is not dangerous. The problem is rotting piles: as they break down they release hydrogen sulfide (H2S), the rotten-egg smell, which can irritate eyes and lungs. Avoid old brown windrows, keep small children and anyone with asthma clear of them, and favor beaches that were cleaned that morning. Our 7-day per-beach forecast flags incoming pulses so you can time a clean window instead of guessing.

sections.h2: How the 2026 map is measured — not guessed
sections.text: Detection starts from the AFAI (Alternative Floating Algae Index), the same spectral signature NOAA and Copernicus satellites use to spot floating sargassum from orbit. We sample it directly offshore of each beach — not a regional average — four times a day, then project each detected mat forward using wind and surface-current data, since sargassum drifts at roughly 2.5% of wind speed plus the local current. Seaweed already on the sand decays by exponential persistence unless fresh material lands. Reports from people standing on the beach correct the satellite when clouds get in the way. We backtest the forecast against what satellites later observed and publish the accuracy openly: days 1–3 verify best, days 4–7 show the trend, and we say so on every page. The data is never hand-edited or filtered for resorts.

sections.h2: Where to go instead on a bad day — and how to get alerts
sections.text: The Mexican Caribbean gives you escape routes the moment your beach goes brown. If the Cancun hotel zone wakes up bad, Playa Norte on Isla Mujeres is usually clean — a short ferry away. From Playa del Carmen, the Cozumel ferry puts you half an hour from Palancar on the seaweed-free west coast. Holbox, up in Gulf waters, barely sees sargassum at all. Inland, a cenote or the ruins make an easy plan B for the heavy days. You can read the live map free every morning. If you'd rather not refresh it yourself, the one-time PASS (USD 5.99 / 11.99 / 19.99, no subscription) adds push alerts the moment your beach changes status, a daily clean-beach recommendation, and a morning brief — so the Watcher tells you where the water is clean before you're even awake.

faq.q: Where is the live Cancun sargassum map for 2026?
faq.a: Right here. This page is the live Cancun seaweed map for 2026: an interactive, color-coded view of every beach from Holbox to Mahahual, updated four times a day from Copernicus and NOAA satellite imagery. Tap any beach for its status today, a 0–100 Beach Score and a 7-day forecast. It changes when the sea changes — check it the morning you go.

faq.q: How often is the seaweed map updated?
faq.a: Four times a day. Each satellite pass refreshes the AFAI reading offshore of every tracked beach, and the 7-day forecast is recalculated daily with the newest data. Every beach displays the exact time of its last update and a confidence level, so you always know how fresh the reading you're looking at really is — no stale averages, no guessing.

faq.q: Will there be a lot of sargassum in Cancun in 2026?
faq.a: The Atlantic belt is large again, so expect a real season April–September, heaviest May–July, with November–March mostly clean. But volume on any single beach swings daily with wind and current. Rather than predict the whole year, we track each beach live and forecast seven days out — check your beach's status today on the map above.

faq.q: Is the seaweed in Cancun dangerous?
faq.a: Fresh sargassum on the sand is harmless. The risk is rotting piles, which release hydrogen sulfide (H2S) — the rotten-egg smell — that can irritate eyes and airways. Avoid old brown windrows, keep children and asthmatics clear, and pick beaches cleaned that morning. The map flags avoid-status beaches so you can steer toward clean, freshly-raked sand.

faq.q: Which Riviera Maya beaches have the least sargassum in 2026?
faq.a: The ones not facing east: Playa Norte on Isla Mujeres, Holbox, and Cozumel's west coast (Palancar) almost never get sargassum. In the Cancun–Tulum corridor, sheltered Akumal and Puerto Morelos pile up far less than open beaches. The live map ranks them cleanest-first each day, so you can confirm which are seaweed-free right now.

faq.q: Does this map cover Playa del Carmen and Tulum too?
faq.a: Yes. The Cancun seaweed map covers the whole Riviera Maya — Cancun, Puerto Morelos, Playa del Carmen, Akumal, Xpu-Ha, Tulum, Cozumel, Isla Mujeres, Holbox and Mahahual — each beach tracked separately. Tulum and Playa del Carmen face east and take more sargassum, so a per-beach reading matters most there. Tap either on the map for its status today.

extraPages.h1: Cancún Seaweed Today — Live Satellite Sargassum Status
extraPages.intro: Want to know the seaweed conditions in Cancún today? Our live satellite map gives you each Cancún beach a current status — clean, moderate, or avoid — updated today from Copernicus and NOAA imagery, so you can see where the water is clear before you pack the car. Every beach gets a Beach Score from 0 to 100 plus a 7-day forecast, refreshed four times a day. Unlike crowd-sourced photo apps that show you yesterday's guess, we read the actual sargassum signal (AFAI) from orbit — measured, not guessed. When clouds block the view, we tell you that too, instead of pretending. Think of it as a watcher that scans the Caribbean while you sleep and reports each morning which Cancún beaches are worth your day. No invented numbers, no fake reviews — just the satellite, honestly read.

sections.h2: Which Cancún beaches are clean today?
sections.text: Sargassum never lands evenly along the Cancún Hotel Zone — one stretch can be clear while the next is buried. Our map scores each beach individually so you don't gamble. The northern beaches around Punta Cancún and Playa Caracol, where the coastline turns to face the calmer bay, often hold a cleaner status than the open east-facing shore, but this shifts with wind and current — which is exactly why you check the live status today rather than trust a fixed reputation. Open the map, look for the green (clean) and amber (moderate) markers, and avoid the red ones. Each marker carries today's Beach Score and a short read of conditions, drawn straight from the latest satellite pass — not from a photo someone posted last week.

sections.h2: When does the seaweed clear in Cancún?
sections.text: Sargassum arrivals come in pulses, not a steady tide. A beach can be hit one morning and noticeably cleaner two or three days later once a band drifts past and crews clear the wrack line. That's why a single status reading isn't enough — you want the trend. Our 7-day per-beach forecast projects how each Cancún beach is likely to move from clean to moderate to avoid, so you can time a beach day or a snorkeling trip for the clearest window. The forecast leans on satellite-tracked sargassum offshore plus current and wind drift. We're honest about uncertainty: the further out the day, the wider the margin, and cloudy passes lower our confidence. We show that confidence rather than hide it.

sections.h2: Where to go instead when Cancún is hit
sections.text: When the whole Cancún strip shows moderate or avoid, you have nearby options — and the same map covers them. Isla Mujeres, sheltered on its western side, frequently stays cleaner than the mainland's east-facing beaches, and the protected coves toward Puerto Morelos can ride out a pulse the open shore can't. Rather than drive blind, scan the live status across the Riviera Maya and pick the highest Beach Score within your range for the day. Because every location refreshes on the same four-times-daily satellite cycle, you're comparing real conditions side by side, not reputations. The goal is simple: one glance, and you know which water is worth the trip this morning — measured from orbit, reported plainly.

faq.q: How do I check seaweed in Cancún today?
faq.a: Open the live map and find your beach — each one shows a current status (clean, moderate, or avoid) and a Beach Score updated today from satellite imagery, so you know conditions before you leave.

faq.q: How often is the Cancún sargassum data updated?
faq.a: Four times a day. We pull fresh Copernicus and NOAA AFAI satellite imagery and re-score every beach, so the status reflects recent conditions rather than an old photo or a single daily guess.

faq.q: Are all Cancún beaches affected the same way?
faq.a: No. Sargassum lands unevenly, so one beach can be clean while a neighbor is buried. That's why we score each beach separately instead of giving the whole city one rating.

faq.q: What happens when clouds block the satellite?
faq.a: We tell you. Cloud cover lowers our confidence for that pass, and we show that rather than inventing a clean reading. Honesty about uncertainty is core to how the Watcher works.

faq.q: Can I see if Cancún will be clear later this week?
faq.a: Yes. Each beach has a 7-day forecast projecting how its status is likely to shift, so you can time a beach or snorkeling day for the clearest window. Confidence narrows the further out you look.

faq.q: How much does access cost?
faq.a: A one-time PASS, starting at USD 5.99 — no subscription. It unlocks per-beach Beach Scores and the 7-day forecast across Cancún and the Riviera Maya.

extraPages.h1: Tulum Sargassum Today — Live Beach Status by Satellite
extraPages.intro: Planning a beach or photo day in Tulum and want to know the sargassum situation today? Our live satellite map gives each Tulum beach a current status — clean, moderate, or avoid — updated today from Copernicus and NOAA imagery, so you can find clear turquoise water before you arrive. Every beach carries a Beach Score from 0 to 100 and a 7-day forecast, refreshed four times a day. Instead of relying on a stranger's photo from last week, we read the actual sargassum signal (AFAI) from orbit — measured, not guessed. When clouds block the satellite's view, we say so rather than fake a clean reading. Picture a watcher scanning the Caribbean overnight, then telling you each morning which Tulum beaches are postcard-clear and which to skip. No invented percentages, no fake testimonials — just the satellite, read honestly, so your photos and your swim aren't ruined by a brown shoreline.

sections.h2: Which Tulum beaches are clear for photos today?
sections.text: Tulum's long beach is not one condition — the public stretch, the hotel zone, and the quieter southern sands toward Sian Ka'an can each show a different status on the same morning. For the clean, golden-and-turquoise shots Tulum is famous for, you need the specific beach, not the town's overall reputation. Our map scores each section individually so you can head straight to the clearest sand. Look for green (clean) and amber (moderate) markers and steer clear of red, and check the Beach Score on each before you commit your morning light to it. Conditions can flip beach to beach, so a quick scan of today's live status beats driving the coast hoping the next stretch is better.

sections.h2: When will the sargassum clear in Tulum?
sections.text: Sargassum reaches Tulum in waves rather than a constant tide, so a beach that's covered today can clear within a few days once a band drifts on and crews rake the wrack line. Because timing matters for a photo trip, we give each beach a 7-day forecast that projects its likely move between clean, moderate, and avoid — letting you book your shoot or beach day for the best window. The forecast tracks sargassum offshore by satellite and factors in current and wind drift. We stay honest about the limits: confidence is highest for the next day or two and widens further out, and cloudy passes reduce certainty. We show you that confidence level instead of pretending every day is a sure bet.

sections.h2: Where to go instead when Tulum is buried
sections.text: If the Tulum coast reads mostly moderate or avoid, the same map covers the rest of the Riviera Maya so you can pivot fast. Sheltered spots and cenotes inland stay sargassum-free year-round, and beaches toward Akumal or the protected bays north can hold a cleaner status when the open Tulum shore takes a hit. Rather than guess, scan the live status across the region and pick the highest Beach Score within driving range for the day. Every location updates on the same four-times-daily satellite cycle, so you're comparing real conditions, not reputations or old reviews. One look tells you where the clear water is this morning — measured from orbit, reported plainly, with no invented numbers.

faq.q: How do I check sargassum in Tulum today?
faq.a: Open the live map and find your beach — each shows a current status (clean, moderate, or avoid) and a Beach Score updated today from satellite imagery, so you know conditions before you go.

faq.q: How often is the Tulum sargassum data refreshed?
faq.a: Four times a day. We pull fresh Copernicus and NOAA AFAI satellite imagery and re-score every beach, so the status reflects recent conditions rather than an old photo or one daily guess.

faq.q: Is all of Tulum beach affected the same?
faq.a: No. The public beach, hotel zone, and southern stretch can each show different conditions on the same morning, so we score each section separately instead of rating the whole town at once.

faq.q: Will my Tulum photos be ruined by seaweed?
faq.a: Not if you check first. The map flags clean beaches today and forecasts the clearest window this week, so you can aim your morning light at turquoise water instead of a brown wrack line.

faq.q: What about when clouds block the satellite?
faq.a: We tell you. Cloud cover lowers our confidence for that pass, and we show that rather than inventing a clean reading. Being honest about uncertainty is core to how the Watcher works.

faq.q: How much does it cost to see the full forecast?
faq.a: A one-time PASS, from USD 5.99 — no subscription. It unlocks per-beach Beach Scores and the 7-day forecast across Tulum and the wider Riviera Maya.
